questions.question: Define an agent and agent function in the context of AI. How does it differ from an agent program?
keyPoints: Agent: entity that perceives environment through sensors and acts through actuators
Agent function: mathematical mapping from percept sequence to action
Agent program: actual implementation/code of agent function
Agent function is abstract specification, agent program is concrete implementation

questions.question: Explain the PEAS framework for specifying task environments with an example of a medical diagnosis system.
keyPoints: P-Performance measure: accuracy of diagnosis, patient health
E-Environment: patient records, symptoms, tests
A-Actuators: display diagnosis, prescribe treatment
S-Sensors: keyboard input, test results, medical history

questions.question: Differentiate between fully observable and partially observable environments. Give one example of each.
keyPoints: Fully observable: complete state accessible via sensors
Partially observable: incomplete state due to limitations or noise
Example fully observable: chess with perfect information
Example partially observable: poker (opponent cards hidden)

questions.question: Compare and contrast deterministic and stochastic environments with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Deterministic: next state completely determined by current state and action
Stochastic: next state has probability distribution
Example deterministic: vacuum cleaner in known grid
Example stochastic: driving in traffic with unpredictable drivers

questions.question: What are the properties of a dynamic environment? Give an example of a dynamic agent environment.
keyPoints: Environment changes while agent is deciding/deliberating
Requires real-time response to changes
Performance measure may change over time
Example: autonomous vehicle driving in traffic, online auction system

questions.question: Explain the difference between simple reflex agent and model-based reflex agent.
keyPoints: Simple reflex: uses condition-action rules based on current percept only
Model-based: maintains internal state to track unobservable aspects
Model-based uses world model (how world evolves, how actions affect it)
Example: reflex vacuum vs vacuum that remembers cleaned areas

questions.question: Define rationality. What factors determine the rationality of an agent?
keyPoints: Rationality: selecting actions that maximize expected performance
Factors: performance measure, prior knowledge, percept sequence, available actions
Rationality depends on what agent should achieve
Autonomy: extent to which agent relies on own experience vs built-in knowledge

questions.question: Explain the four main types of intelligent agents: simple reflex, model-based reflex, goal-based, and utility-based. Discuss their increasing sophistication with a suitable application example for each.
keyPoints: Simple reflex: condition-action rules, current percept only, e.g., thermostat
Model-based reflex: internal state + world model, e.g., vacuum cleaner with map
Goal-based: considers future goals, plans actions, e.g., route planning GPS
Utility-based: maximizes utility function, handles tradeoffs, e.g., autonomous vehicle choosing route balancing time/safety/cost
Show progression from reactive to deliberative reasoning

questions.question: Classify and explain all the properties of task environments with respect to: (a) Observable/Partially Observable, (b) Deterministic/Stochastic, (c) Static/Dynamic, (d) Discrete/Continuous, (e) Single/Multi-agent, (f) Episodic/Sequential. Provide a real-world example for each property.
keyPoints: (a) Observable: full state visible, Partially: limited sensors - Chess vs Poker
(b) Deterministic: predictable outcomes, Stochastic: probabilistic - Calculator vs Dice game
(c) Static: unchanged during decision, Dynamic: changes - Crossword vs Stock market
(d) Discrete: finite states/actions, Continuous: infinite - Chess vs Temperature control
(e) Single: one agent, Multi: multiple interacting - Solitaire vs Chess
(f) Episodic: independent episodes, Sequential: current affects future - Email classifier vs Game playing

questions.question: Design an agent for playing chess. Use the PEAS framework to specify its task environment and identify the type of environment (fully/partially observable, deterministic/stochastic, etc.).
keyPoints: Performance measure: win/draw/lose ratio, game outcome
Environment: chess board state, opponent moves, time clock
Actuators: move piece, claim draw, resign
Sensors: board configuration input, clock
Fully observable (board visible), Deterministic (rules fixed), Sequential (moves affect game), Discrete (finite moves), Static (board only changes on moves), Two-player Multi-agent

questions.question: What is autonomy in agents? How does a rational agent balance autonomy with predefined knowledge? Discuss with examples of high-autonomy and low-autonomy agents.
keyPoints: Autonomy: extent to which agent relies on own experience vs built-in knowledge
High autonomy: learns from environment, adapts, e.g., self-driving car exploring new routes
Low autonomy: follows predefined rules, e.g., basic thermostat with fixed temperature thresholds
Rational agent uses prior knowledge but can learn/adapt
Balance: sufficient built-in knowledge to start, learn from experience to improve
